title: Conjecture 2 (Pavez-Signé, Piga, Sanhueza-Matamala [9], Conjecture 16)
statement_text: For every $k \geq 2$ and every function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $n \leq f(n) \leq r(K_n)$ for all $n$, there is a sequence $(G_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ of $k$-connected graphs such that $|V(G_n)| = n$ and $r(G_n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
context_text: Pavez-Signé, Piga and Sanhueza-Matamala had shown that every reasonable growth rate between $n$ and $r(K_n)$ is achieved (up to a multiplicative constant) by a sequence of connected graphs, and conjectured that the graphs could additionally be taken to have arbitrarily large connectivity. The present paper was written specifically to prove this conjecture.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture in full: for every $k \geq 2$ there exists a constant $C_k > 0$ such that for every admissible $f$ a sequence of $k$-connected $n$-vertex graphs with $r(G_n) \leq C_k f(n)$ is constructed (Theorem 3).
notes: PDF source; mathematical notation appears clean and unambiguous.
paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity

title: Question 9 (Pavez-Signé, Piga, Sanhueza-Matamala [9], Question 13)
statement_text: Is there an $n_0 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$, $R_n = [n, r(K_n)]$ and $\mathcal{R}^c_n = [\lceil \tfrac{4}{3}n \rceil - 1,\, r(K_n)]$?
context_text: Here $R_n = \{r(G) : |V(G)| = n\}$ and $\mathcal{R}^c_n = \{r(G) : |V(G)| = n,\, G \text{ connected}\}$ are the sets of Ramsey numbers of $n$-vertex (connected) graphs. Known inclusions give $R_n \subseteq [n, r(K_n)]$ and $\mathcal{R}^c_n \subseteq [\lceil \tfrac{4}{3}n\rceil - 1, r(K_n)]$; an affirmative answer would mean every integer in these intervals is a Ramsey number, allowing any growth rate to be achieved exactly. The authors note the answer may well be negative.
paper_contribution: This paper does not make direct progress on the question; it is cited as motivation for understanding the fine structure of Ramsey number sets.
notes: PDF source; the ceiling notation and fraction are slightly ambiguous in the extracted text but appear consistent with the surrounding discussion.
paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity

title: Conjecture 10
statement_text: For all functions $f(n) : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with $n \leq f(n) \leq r(K_n)$ and for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a sequence of graphs $(G_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ satisfying $|V(G_n)| = n$ and $(1-\varepsilon)f(n) \leq r(G_n) \leq (1+\varepsilon)f(n)$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
context_text: The paper's main theorem achieves every growth rate up to a multiplicative constant $C_k$; Conjecture 10 asks whether the multiplicative gaps in $\mathcal{R}^c_n$ can be made $1+o(1)$, i.e., whether any prescribed growth rate can be approximated arbitrarily closely. The authors suggest that edge-deletion results (such as a conjecture of Wigderson) might provide a route to this.
notes: PDF source; mathematical notation is clean.
paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity

title: Wigderson Edge-Deletion Conjecture
statement_text: Deleting an edge from a graph can change the Ramsey number by at most a constant factor.
context_text: This conjecture of Wigderson (arXiv:2208.11181, 2022) is mentioned in the concluding remarks as a potential tool for approaching Conjecture 10: if for dense graphs there is always an edge $e$ whose deletion changes the Ramsey number by at most a $1+o(1)$ factor, that would give a path toward achieving prescribed growth rates exactly.
paper_contribution: This paper makes no direct progress on the conjecture; it is mentioned only as a possible approach to Conjecture 10.
notes: PDF source; stated in prose without a labelled environment. The precise formal statement (e.g., whether 'constant' means uniform over all graphs) is not given in this paper.
paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity
